name: Ruthenium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Ruthenium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Ruthenium oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for efficient Ruthenium oxide absorption with minimal substrate interaction

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise Ruthenium surface cleaning

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for Ruthenium oxide removal without bulk material damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled Ruthenium oxide ablation

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Ruthenium surface treatment

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete Ruthenium oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform Ruthenium surface cleaning

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals like Ruthenium; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and heat input to avoid melting (melting point ~2334°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and general metal cleaning guidelines (e.g., SPIE proceedings on laser ablation).

scan_speed.description: Effective for contamination removal; slower for heavy contamination (100-500 mm/s), faster for light (500-2000 mm/s). Recommended 500 mm/s for Ruthenium to ensure uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation due to its thermal conductivity (~120 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax and CleanLaser equipment manuals.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Ruthenium cleaning to ablate contaminants while minimizing heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications. 20 ns recommended for low thermal damage on high-reflectivity surfaces. Source: Laser physics studies (e.g., Applied Surface Science journal on ns vs. ps ablation thresholds for noble metals).

repetition_rate.description: Standard for pulsed cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat buildup on Ruthenium's reflective surface (reflectivity ~60% at 1064 nm). 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: high freq increases overlap heating. Source: Academic literature (e.g., Optics & Lasers in Engineering on repetition rate effects in metal cleaning).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation without substrate damage; Ruthenium damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to high melting point. Recommended 1 J/cm² for safe cleaning of light contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies (e.g., Journal of Applied Physics on noble metal thresholds; conservative estimate for Ru similar to Pt/Rh).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (50-200 μm) for fine surface prep on Ruthenium components; larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution on small industrial parts. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers for precision cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers to avoid damage. Recommended 2 passes for Ruthenium to ensure complete removal while respecting thermal properties. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of catalytic surfaces in chemical engineering reports).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; 50% optimal for Ruthenium to prevent uneven heating on reflective surfaces. Typical range 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization studies (e.g., Journal of Laser Applications on scan overlap for metal surface treatment).
